node.title: Uber | System Design | Design a Traffic Violation System

topic.title: Uber | System Design | Design a Traffic Violation System

post.content:
Design a traffic violation system (that detects vehicle collission, exceed speed limit,  jumping the red light ) 
**Problem Statement**

This system is getting images from 1000s of cameras placed in different parts of a city. Each camera is separated by 500 metres, clicks 10 frames per second and each frame is about 20 KB in size.  There are services available to give vehicle details given vehicle no. There are different  services available for image processing that can take in set of frames (images) and return back the violation type and vehicles number plates that are in violation. Goal is to send the control room details regarding where violation occurred and vehicles involved and ability to trace back to the images. System must be scalable and fault tolerant as more cities can be added and more cameras can be added.

1. How will you store and manage the querying of the images - what are your technology choices?
2. In the case of speed violations you might need to combine images from one or more cameras as you can't achieve speed violation from a single camera.
3. How will you prevent or handle out of order messages if any

Need more requirements:
- How fast is the processing service that returns back violation type and vehicle number plates?
- How fast is the service that returns vehicle details given vehicle number? What details does it contain?
- What are bandwidth/storage requirements?

For 1000 cameras we need at least 1.5Gbps network throughput, and it scales quite fast, so I imagine that each city should split it up to districts, with every district monitoring it's own cameras, maybe with a bit of redundancy: cameras should overlap between districts, e.g. for speed violations. However, need to watch out for cases when accidents happened at the edge of districts: some system has to check whether violation for the car already exists in adjacent region.

Since cameras are separated and not directly connected to each other, we either have to store them in a graph-like structure, and/or have coordinates assigned for every camera id.
This would allow us to get nearby camera ids given coordinates and/or camera id.
For the Feed we'd like to collect image id, timestamp with milliseconds, image file hash (md5/sha1).

Images should be uploaded into Storage service by pathname: `camera-id/year/month/day/timestamp-with-milliseconds-hash`. 
We also should have access to Vehicle ID through Vehicle service and most likely Violations service that reference vehicle id, violation type, image ids describing the incident.

To go through all these systems, I assume that realistically you should be able to deploy as many image processing services as needed, depending on constraints. If we need to handle 200MB of images per second then we should be able to scale the needed service. Otherwise we have to discard images that doesn't contain any cars in some sort of intelligent way or run some sort of pre-processing where we can reconstruct car trajectories, their speed at specific periods of time, and location.

Overall, we should have 6 main entities we can split our system into:
- Camera (camera_id, coordinates, adjacent camera_ids)
- Feed (image_id, timestamp_with_ms, file_hash, detected_vehicle_ids): we might split `detected_vehicle_ids` into DetectedVehicle (image_id, vehicle_id)
- Storage (camera_id, url_to_file)
- Vehicle (vehicle_id)
- ViolationScan (violation_scan_id, image_id, type: "speed_limit", status: "queued"): scan could have multiple `image_ids`
- Violation (violation_id, vehicle_ids, violation_type, image_ids): feel free to split it up into ViolationVehicle (violation_id, vehicle_id) and ViolationImage (violation_id, image_id), and then we can join them into single Violation object containing details of the incident.

Imagine we have an image containing a car.
1. Image file should have timestamp
2. Camera uploads the image to some sort of Ingestion service that handles two things: enqueue image metadata to be stored into `Feed` and the file uploaded to `Storage`
3. Feed simply saves the Feed (image_id, timestamp_with_ms, file_hash)
4. Storage must trigger API call to Violation service, which then runs few processes:
4. a. Run violation scan to determine if image or a set of images contains violation (create ViolationScan and update it on subsequent processes)
4. b. Queue up an update to Feed with new details: `detected_vehicle_id`
4. c. Create Violation (violation_id, vehicle_ids, violation_type, image_ids) entity
5. Another process (could be implemented on Feed) runs periodically (every minute or so) to grab images from adjacent cameras that contain same `detected_vehicle_id`, and runs ViolationScan (type: "speed_limit")

Now, lets talk about questions:

> 1. How will you store and manage the querying of the images - what are your technology choices?

Obviously, we need a lot of bandwidth to be available for our ingestion service, 200MB/s (1.5Gbps) per 1000 cameras, so we can scale our ingestion instances across the network endpoints.
200MB/s is pretty low even for a low-end SSD. We can scale up to 10000 cameras stored by a single Storage instance. In a scenario we need more, we can easily scale Storage instances and LB them using `camera_id` hashes. We can also use Redis to buffer 1-2 minutes of footage (30-50GB/s, but RAM could be expensive) and have background processes which take older keys and store them to SSD over time.
If our violation processing is fast enough, we can even retrieve images from Redis directly, with Storage acting as longterm storage and buffer in case Violation service is down. Processing would stop for a while but we would be able to resume it without losing consistency.

> 2. In the case of speed violations you might need to combine images from one or more cameras as you can't achieve speed violation from a single camera.

This is where I'd ask a question whether or not we can detect `vehicle_id` using a service before running ViolationScan, and then send to API only images that actually contain identified vehicles. I'd expect this would reduce load by a huge margin. I outlined this in the step 5, assuming that Violation would identify vehicle_ids which we can backfill later and run scan again using combined images.

> 3. How will you prevent or handle out of order messages if any

Since every image should have a timestamp and camera id, we should be able to generate consistent events timeline, even if we upload images much later than they were taken.

That's about it, feel free to discuss/suggest improvements.
I have actually found a video for this _exact_ problem with coverage from a FAANG senior engineer:\nhttps://youtu.be/cNMQAh7UVn0
[@Hack3r](https://leetcode.com/Hack3r) have you found the solution for this anywhere, this problem statement is really intresting\n
